---
title: "Inference Quantization Explained"
---

Inference quantization is the process of reducing the numerical precision used to store a model's weights (and sometimes activations) when running inference. Instead of using full 32-bit floating-point numbers, the weights are compressed into smaller representations — fewer bits per number.

The trade-off is fundamental:

```text
Higher precision ◄───────────────────────────────► Lower precision
┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐
│ FP32   │ │ FP16   │ │ Q8   │ │ Q6   │ │ Q4   │ │ Q2   │
└────────┘ └────────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘
32 bit     16 bit     8 bit    ~6 bit   ~4 bit   ~2 bit

More VRAM ──────────────────────────────────────────► Less VRAM
Slower    ──────────────────────────────────────────► Faster
Best      ──────────────────────────────────────────► Quality loss
quality
```

## Why Quantize?

A 7B parameter model in FP32 needs ~28 GB of VRAM. Quantize the same model to Q4 and it fits in ~4 GB — runnable on a laptop GPU. The model's behavior is *approximated* using less information per weight, but for many tasks the quality drop is surprisingly small.

## How a Single Weight Is Stored

Conceptually, imagine one weight value `w = 0.7384921…`:

```text
FP32 : sign │ 8-bit exponent │ 23-bit mantissa → very precise
FP16 : sign │ 5-bit exponent │ 10-bit mantissa → still smooth
Q8   : maps weight into one of 256 integer levels → coarse but small
Q4   : maps weight into one of 16 integer levels → very coarse
```

Integer-style quantization (`Q*`) typically stores weights in blocks: a group of weights shares a scale factor (and sometimes a zero-point), so the integers can be dequantized back to float at runtime.

## The Specific Options in Your Dropdown

| Option | Family | What it is |
|----------|--------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| FP32 | IEEE float | Full precision, training-grade. Rarely needed for inference. |
| FP16 | IEEE float | Half precision. Standard "high-quality" inference baseline. |
| NVFP6 | NVIDIA microscaling | A 6-bit floating-point format from NVIDIA's MX (Microscaling) family, designed for Blackwell-era GPUs. |
| NVFP4 | NVIDIA microscaling | The 4-bit cousin — even smaller, optimized for hardware tensor cores. |
| Q8 | Integer quant | 8-bit integer quantization. Near-FP16 quality, half the VRAM. Very safe default. |
| Q7 | Integer quant | 7-bit — uncommon, intermediate point. |
| Q6K | llama.cpp "K-quant" | 6-bit with the *K-quant* scheme: weights grouped into super-blocks, with smarter per-block scales for better quality at the same bit budget. |

The K suffix (`Q6K`, `Q4_K_M`, `Q5_K_S`, etc.) comes from the llama.cpp ecosystem and signals improved block-wise quantization that preserves quality much better than naive Q4/Q6.

## Practical Picking Guide

```text
Quality Footprint
 ▲ ▲
 FP16   │ ●                                      │ ● FP32
 Q8     │ ●                                      │ ● FP16
 Q6K    │ ● ◄── sweet spot for many users        │ ● Q8
 Q5_K_M │ ●                                      │ ● Q6K
 Q4_K_M │ ● ◄── common laptop sweet spot         │ ● Q4_K_M
 Q3     │ ●                                      │ ● Q3
 Q2     │ ● noticeable degradation               │ ● Q2
        └──────────────────────────────────►     └──────────────────►
```

A common rule of thumb: Q6K and Q5_K_M are often indistinguishable from FP16 for chat-style use, while Q4_K_M is the most popular "fits-on-consumer-hardware" choice. Drop below Q4 only when memory is truly tight.

For your radiology AI work, if you ever serve multimodal or vision-language models locally, Q6K or Q8 is a reasonable default — diagnostic-adjacent reasoning tends to be more sensitive to quantization than casual chat, so erring on the higher-precision side is wise.
